definition: Combining multiple data points into a single summary value such as a sum, mean, or count.
Identifying data points that deviate significantly from expected patterns or distributions.
An ordered collection of elements of the same type, foundational to numerical computing.
The sum of all values in a dataset divided by the count of those values.
An algorithm computing gradients through a neural network to update weights during training.
A simple reference model used to measure whether a more complex model adds real value.
A subset of training data processed together in one forward and backward pass of a model.
Updating the probability of a hypothesis as new evidence is incorporated using Bayes' theorem.
Systematic error in a model that causes it to consistently miss the true value in one direction.
A prediction task with exactly two possible output categories, such as spam or not spam.
A variable representing discrete groups or labels rather than continuous numeric values.
The geometric center of a cluster, used as a reference point in algorithms like k-means.
A dataset condition where one category has significantly more samples than others.
A supervised learning task that predicts which category a new observation belongs to.
An unsupervised method grouping similar data points together without predefined labels.
A table comparing predicted vs actual class labels to evaluate a classification model.
A numeric variable that can take any value within a range, such as height or temperature.
A statistical measure of how strongly two variables move together, ranging from -1 to 1.
A technique splitting data into multiple train-test folds to produce a more reliable evaluation.
Comma-Separated Values — a plain text format for storing tabular data, one row per line.
The process of detecting and correcting errors, inconsistencies, and missing values in a dataset.
The gradual change in input data distribution over time that degrades model performance.
A centralized repository storing raw, unstructured data at scale for future processing.
An automated sequence of steps that ingests, transforms, and loads data from source to destination.
A structured storage system optimized for querying and analyzing large volumes of historical data.
A two-dimensional, labeled data structure with columns of potentially different types, as in pandas.
A flowchart-like model that splits data based on feature conditions to make predictions.
Reducing the number of features in a dataset while preserving as much information as possible.
A function describing the frequency of values across all possible outcomes in a dataset.
Exploratory Data Analysis — the initial investigation of data using statistics and visualizations.
One complete pass of the entire training dataset through a machine learning model.
Extract, Transform, Load — the process of moving and reshaping data between systems.
A quantitative measure used to assess how well a model performs on a given task.
The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both in a single performance metric.
An individual measurable variable used as input to a machine learning model.
Creating or transforming input variables to improve a model's predictive power.
A score indicating how much each input variable contributed to a model's predictions.
An optimization algorithm iteratively adjusting model parameters to minimize a loss function.
The verified correct labels or values used to train and evaluate a machine learning model.
A portion of data withheld from training and used only for final model evaluation.
A configuration value set before training that controls how a model learns, not what it learns.
A statistical method determining whether observed data provides evidence against a null assumption.
Filling in missing values in a dataset using statistical estimates like the mean or median.
The phase where a trained model generates predictions on new, unseen input data.
The difference between the 75th and 25th percentile, measuring statistical spread robustly.
The target output value a supervised learning model is trained to predict.
A hyperparameter controlling how large each step is during gradient descent optimization.
A function measuring the difference between predicted and actual values during model training.
The middle value in an ordered dataset, robust to outliers unlike the arithmetic mean.
An absent data point in a dataset, represented as null or NaN, requiring handling before modeling.
A mathematical function learned from data that maps inputs to predicted outputs.
A condition where two or more input features are highly correlated, distorting model coefficients.
A symmetric, bell-shaped probability distribution where most values cluster around the mean.
Rescaling feature values to a standard range — typically 0 to 1 — before model training.
The default assumption of no effect or no difference that a statistical test attempts to reject.
A data point that differs markedly from other observations and may indicate noise or a real anomaly.
When a model learns the training data too precisely, performing poorly on new unseen data.
The probability of observing results at least as extreme as those seen, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
A Python library providing DataFrame structures for data manipulation and analysis.
The value below which a given percentage of observations in a dataset fall.
A chained sequence of preprocessing and modeling steps that can be fit and applied as a unit.
The proportion of positive predictions that were actually correct out of all positive predictions made.
The output a trained model generates when given a new input it has not seen before.
All data transformation steps applied before feeding data into a machine learning model.
An ensemble model combining many decision trees to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting.
The proportion of actual positives that a model correctly identified out of all real positives.
A supervised learning task predicting a continuous numeric output from input features.
A technique penalizing model complexity during training to reduce overfitting.
A subset of observations drawn from a larger population for analysis or model training.
A systematic error occurring when the collected data does not represent the full population.
A measure of the asymmetry of a distribution — positive skew has a longer right tail.
Dividing a dataset into separate portions for training a model and evaluating its performance.
A measure of how spread out values are relative to the mean in a dataset.
Training a model on labeled data where the correct output is known for each input.
The output column a model is trained to predict, also called the dependent variable.
The labeled dataset used to fit a machine learning model's parameters.
When a model is too simple to capture patterns in the data, performing poorly on both train and test sets.
Training a model on unlabeled data to discover hidden structure or patterns without guidance.
A model's sensitivity to fluctuations in training data — high variance models tend to overfit.
A one-dimensional array of numbers representing a data point or feature set in a model.
Representing data graphically to reveal patterns, trends, and outliers at a glance.
A learned parameter in a model that scales the importance of an input feature.
A server that acts as the single entry point for all client requests to backend services.
The process of verifying who a user is before granting access to a system.
Determining what actions or resources an authenticated user is permitted to access.
A retry strategy that progressively increases wait time between failed attempts.
The process of marking cached data as stale so it gets refreshed on the next request.
A pattern that stops requests to a failing service to prevent cascading failures.
The ability of a system to handle multiple tasks at overlapping points in time.
Reusing a set of established database connections instead of creating new ones per request.
Server-sent HTTP headers that control which origins are allowed to access its resources.
Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations performed on persistent data.
A versioned script that applies incremental changes to a database schema over time.
A state where two or more processes block each other indefinitely waiting for resources.
Releasing a new version of an application to a server or hosting environment.
Serverless code executed at CDN nodes geographically close to the user for low latency.
A specific URL path in an API that accepts requests and returns a defined response.
A configuration value stored outside source code and injected at runtime per environment.
A database column that references the primary key of another table to enforce relationships.
A query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data shape they need.
Adding more server instances to distribute load rather than upgrading a single machine.
Numeric codes in server responses indicating the result of a request, such as 200 or 404.
A unique identifier sent with a request to prevent duplicate processing on retry.
A database structure that speeds up row lookups by storing a sorted reference to column values.
Incoming network traffic to a service, or the Kubernetes resource that routes external requests.
A test verifying that multiple components or services work correctly when combined.
JSON Web Token — a compact signed token used to transmit verified user identity claims.
The time delay between a request being sent and the first byte of a response being received.
A system that distributes incoming requests across multiple server instances evenly.
Recording application events and errors to a persistent store for monitoring and debugging.
A buffer that decouples services by storing messages until the receiver is ready to process them.
A small, independently deployable service responsible for one specific business capability.
A function that runs between receiving a request and sending a response to process or transform it.
A performance bug where fetching a list triggers an extra query per item instead of one batch.
Organizing database tables to reduce redundancy by storing each fact in exactly one place.
An open standard allowing users to grant apps limited access to their accounts without sharing passwords.
The ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs: logs, metrics, traces.
Updating the UI immediately on user action before the server confirms success.
Object-Relational Mapper — a library that lets you query a database using language objects.
A pagination strategy using a pointer to the last item instead of page numbers for stable results.
The data body carried inside a network request or response, excluding headers.
A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies each row in a database table.
An intermediary server that forwards requests between a client and another server.
The execution strategy a database engine chooses to retrieve data for a given query.
Restricting how many requests a client can make to an API within a given time window.
Copying data across multiple database instances to ensure availability and fault tolerance.
Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for stateless, resource-based APIs.
Reverting a database transaction or deployment to its previous stable state.
A database feature restricting which rows a user can read or modify based on defined policies.
The formal definition of a database's structure: its tables, columns, types, and relationships.
A model where backend code runs in stateless functions managed and scaled by a cloud provider.
Horizontally partitioning a database across multiple machines to distribute load.
Marking a record as deleted with a flag instead of physically removing it from the database.
An attack inserting malicious SQL into a query to manipulate or expose database data.
Server-Sent Events — a protocol for servers to push real-time updates to the browser over HTTP.
A server that stores no client session data between requests — each request is self-contained.
A container for storing user-uploaded files and assets in a cloud storage service.
The number of requests or operations a system can successfully handle per unit of time.
A group of database operations that execute as a single atomic unit — all succeed or all fail.
Time To Live — the duration a cached value or DNS record is considered valid before expiry.
Universally Unique Identifier — a 128-bit value used as a collision-resistant database primary key.
Upgrading a single server's CPU, RAM, or storage capacity to handle more load.
An HTTP callback triggered by an event in one service to notify another in real time.
A protocol providing a persistent, full-duplex communication channel between client and server.
A release strategy that updates a service without any interruption to active users.
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the four guarantees of reliable database transactions.
A scheduled task that runs automatically at defined time intervals on a server.
Designing a system to maintain partial functionality when a component fails.
A periodic request to a service endpoint that confirms it is running and responsive.
An API request that can be safely repeated multiple times without changing the outcome.
A precomputed database query result stored on disk and refreshed on demand.
A persistent connection that pushes database changes to the client as they happen.
Populating a database with initial or test data using a script.
A database operation that inserts a row if it does not exist or updates it if it does.

Identifying data points that deviate significantly from expected patterns or distributions.

A subset of observations drawn from a larger population for analysis or model training.
A systematic error occurring when the collected data does not represent the full population.

When a model is too simple to capture patterns in the data, performing poorly on both train and test sets.
Training a model on unlabeled data to discover hidden structure or patterns without guidance.
A model's sensitivity to fluctuations in training data — high variance models tend to overfit.
A one-dimensional array of numbers representing a data point or feature set in a model.
Representing data graphically to reveal patterns, trends, and outliers at a glance.
A learned parameter in a model that scales the importance of an input feature.
A level in a design system that separates raw tokens from component-level decisions.
A semantic token that references a primitive token and communicates its intended use.
A methodology by Brad Frost organizing UI into atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages.
An unstyled or minimally styled component that provides behavior without visual opinions.
A horizontal rhythm system aligning text and elements to a consistent vertical spacing unit.
A design token defining the corner rounding values available across a system.
A defined viewport width at which the layout adapts to suit a different screen size.
A CSS @layer rule that explicitly controls the order of specificity between style groups.
The complete set of raw color values available in a system before semantic assignment.
A graduated scale of tints and shades for a single hue, typically numbered 50 through 900.
A semantic label describing a color's function — such as surface, on-surface, or error.
The set of props a component exposes to control its appearance and behavior from outside.
A versioned, documented collection of reusable UI components shared across a product or org.
Building complex UI by combining smaller, focused components rather than creating monolithic ones.
A style rule applied only when two or more variant conditions are true simultaneously.
A deliberate system rule that limits choices to maintain consistency across the product.
A product team that uses a design system's components without contributing to its core.
A variable declared with -- prefix in CSS, used to propagate design token values at runtime.
An approach colocating component styles written in JavaScript alongside component logic.
A color scheme variant using dark backgrounds, requiring semantic tokens to switch correctly.
The amount of content and spacing in a UI — compact vs comfortable vs spacious layouts.
The shared visual and interactive vocabulary — principles, patterns, and aesthetics — of a brand.
A named variable storing a single design decision — color, spacing, radius — usable across platforms.
A dedicated website publishing component usage guidelines, props, and live code examples.
A system representing depth through shadow values, communicating component stacking order.
A secondary typeface rendered while a web font loads to prevent invisible or shifted text.
Font sizes that scale smoothly between breakpoints using CSS clamp() instead of fixed steps.
A visible outline appearing on focused interactive elements to aid keyboard navigation.
A set of predefined font size steps — xs through 4xl — forming the typographic range of a system.
An ordered list of typefaces in CSS, falling back to the next if the preferred one is unavailable.
CSS rules applied to the entire document — resets, root variables, and base element defaults.
Fixed spacing between columns in a grid layout that maintains visual separation.
A component providing only logic and accessibility with no default styles applied.
A CSS custom property storing a hue value to drive a dynamic accent color system.
A cohesive set of SVG icons sharing consistent sizing, stroke weight, and visual style.
The ability of a child component to receive token values cascaded from a parent theme.
A component's visual condition in response to user action: hover, focus, active, or disabled.
A semantic surface token — background, surface, overlay — defining stacking context colors.
The vertical space allocated for each line of text, affecting readability and vertical rhythm.
Automated checks enforcing that only approved design tokens are used in component code.
An ARIA attribute that announces dynamic content changes to screen readers automatically.
A design token storing duration and easing values for consistent animation across a system.
Adjusting letter-spacing or weight at different sizes to maintain visual balance of text.
A documented, reusable solution to a recurring design problem within a specific context.
A component that can render as different HTML elements via an 'as' prop without losing its styles.
A raw, context-free token storing a single value — blue-500, space-4 — without semantic meaning.
Applying different styles to a component based on the values of its received props.
A CSS media query respecting the user's OS preference to minimize animation and transitions.
A stylesheet neutralizing browser default styles to create a consistent cross-browser baseline.
A token whose value changes at defined breakpoints to adapt spacing or type for screen size.
A set of predefined spacing values — 4, 8, 12, 16… — used consistently across layout and components.
A color token named by its purpose — danger, success, muted — rather than its raw value.
A design token encoding box-shadow values to represent consistent elevation levels.
A named placeholder inside a component where consumers inject custom content or child elements.
A numeric weight CSS assigns to selectors to decide which rule wins when rules conflict.
An open-source tool that transforms design tokens into platform-specific output files.
Swapping a set of token values to change the visual appearance of a system without touching components.
The automated workflow transforming raw token definitions into CSS variables or JS exports.
A single-purpose CSS class applying one specific style property, as used in Tailwind CSS.
A named style configuration of a component — primary, ghost, destructive — controlled via props.
Tagging system releases with semantic version numbers to manage breaking and non-breaking changes.
An automated test comparing component screenshots to detect unintended visual changes.
A set of named z-index token values — dropdown, modal, toast — to manage stacking order predictably.
Class Variance Authority — a library for defining typed component variants with Tailwind CSS.
Resolving Tailwind conflicts when multiple classes target the same CSS property, using tools like cn().
The rate at which product teams migrate to and consistently use a shared design system.
The defined process for how teams outside the core system team can propose or add components.
An attribute communicating an element's purpose to assistive technologies beyond its HTML tag.
A React API that passes a ref through a component to a DOM element inside it.
A component is open for extension via props but closed for modification of its internal logic.
Making minor visual adjustments — often misalignments of 1–2px — to achieve precise rendering.
A semantic color role representing the background layer on which content and components sit.
Hiding implementation complexity behind a simpler interface to reduce cognitive overhead.
Application Programming Interface — a contract defining how software components communicate.
Operations that run independently without blocking the main thread while waiting to complete.
A single output file combining multiple source modules for efficient browser delivery.
A tool that compiles and combines source files into optimized assets for production.
A layer that stores frequently accessed data to reduce redundant fetching or computation.
A function passed as an argument to another function, invoked after an operation completes.
Content Delivery Network — a distributed server system delivering assets from locations near the user.
Code or logic that executes in the user's browser rather than on the server.
A function that retains access to its outer scope's variables even after that scope has returned.
Dividing a bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand to improve initial load time.
A self-contained, reusable piece of UI that manages its own structure, style, and behavior.
A React component whose input value is driven entirely by state rather than the DOM.
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — a browser security mechanism controlling cross-domain requests.
The algorithm CSS uses to resolve which styles apply when multiple rules target the same element.
A reusable React function prefixed with 'use' that encapsulates stateful logic for sharing.
Delaying a function's execution until a specified time has passed since its last invocation.
A programming style that describes what the UI should look like rather than how to build it.
The second argument to React hooks that controls when an effect or memo should re-run.
A value computed from existing state rather than stored independently in the state tree.
Document Object Model — the tree structure browsers use to represent and manipulate HTML.
Don't Repeat Yourself — a principle advocating for eliminating code duplication through abstraction.
Loading a JavaScript module on demand at runtime rather than at initial bundle load.
An automated test that simulates real user scenarios across the full application stack.
Attaching a single event listener to a parent element to handle events from its children.
The JavaScript runtime mechanism that processes the call stack and task queue sequentially.
An alternative value, component, or behavior used when the primary option fails or is unavailable.
A toggle that enables or disables a feature at runtime without deploying new code.
Creating a personal copy of a repository to develop independently from the original source.
A reusable type definition in TypeScript that works with multiple types via a type parameter.
Automatically updating the browser with code changes without a full page refresh.
Attaching React event listeners to server-rendered HTML to make it interactive on the client.
An operation that produces the same result regardless of how many times it is executed.
A pattern where data is never modified in place — changes produce new copies instead.
A browser API that efficiently detects when elements enter or leave the viewport.
Deferring the load of non-critical resources until they are actually needed by the user.
Moving shared state up to the nearest common ancestor component so siblings can access it.
Automated static analysis that flags code style issues, errors, and anti-patterns.
A CSS rule that applies styles conditionally based on device characteristics like screen width.
Caching the result of a function call so repeated calls with the same input skip recomputation.
A function that intercepts and transforms requests or actions before they reach their destination.
A simulated object or function that replaces a real dependency during testing.
A single repository containing multiple related projects or packages managed together.
Directly modifying existing data in memory rather than creating a new copy of it.
The ?? operator that returns the right-hand value only when the left is null or undefined.
The ?. operator that safely accesses nested properties without throwing if one is undefined.
Dividing a large dataset into discrete pages to reduce load time and improve usability.
Code that implements a modern feature in browsers that do not natively support it.
A React feature that renders a child component into a DOM node outside the parent tree.
Passing data through multiple component layers that don't use it to reach a deep child.
A function with no side effects that always returns the same output for the same input.
A bug where the outcome depends on the unpredictable timing of concurrent operations.
React's algorithm for comparing virtual DOM trees to determine the minimal DOM updates needed.
Restructuring existing code to improve readability or structure without changing its behavior.
A React pattern where a component shares logic by accepting a function as a prop.
A React component updating its output in response to state or prop changes.
JavaScript syntax using ... to collect remaining values into an array or expand iterables.
The context that determines where variables are accessible within a program.
Converting a data structure into a format like JSON for storage or network transmission.
Generating full HTML on the server per request, improving initial load and SEO.
Any operation a function performs beyond returning a value, such as a fetch or DOM mutation.
Storing a piece of data in one place so all parts of the app read from the same state.
A CSS scoring system that determines which rule takes priority when multiple rules conflict.
Pre-rendering pages to static HTML at build time for fast delivery without a server.
A tool for developing and documenting UI components in isolation outside the main app.
A React wrapper that intentionally double-invokes functions to surface side effect bugs.
Limiting a function to execute at most once per defined time interval regardless of calls.
A build optimization that removes unused exported code from the final bundle.
TypeScript's ability to refine a broad type to a more specific one within a conditional block.
A TypeScript type representing a value that can be one of several specified types.
A React form element whose value is managed by the DOM rather than component state.
An in-memory representation of the UI that React diffs against the real DOM before updating.
A sequential chain of network requests where each one waits for the previous to complete.
Google's set of metrics measuring real-world user experience: LCP, INP, and CLS.
A minimal React state management library using a hook-based store without boilerplate.
A React pattern where multiple components share implicit state to form a cohesive unit.
React's built-in mechanism for passing data through the component tree without prop drilling.
A React component that catches JavaScript errors in its subtree and renders a fallback UI.
A bug where a function captures an outdated variable value from a previous render.
A parallel DOM representation that assistive technologies use to interpret the interface.
Using HTML elements that convey meaning about content structure rather than just appearance.
To actively support and argue for something — a design decision, a user need, or a process.
Unclear or incomplete specs that require you to ask questions and make informed assumptions.
Your method or strategy for solving a problem — interviewers love hearing about your approach.
To express your ideas clearly and precisely, especially technical or design decisions.
Able to work independently and make decisions without constant supervision.
To weigh competing priorities — speed vs quality, design vs engineering constraints.
To connect two different worlds — typically design and engineering — reducing friction between them.
To create something starting from nothing rather than extending an existing solution.
A difficulty or obstacle you encountered — often the setup for a strong interview story.
To work jointly with others — designers, engineers, PMs — toward a shared outcome.
Explaining the pros and cons of a decision clearly so stakeholders can choose knowingly.
A development approach that builds UIs as a composition of isolated, reusable components.
The quality of applying the same patterns, styles, and decisions throughout a product.
To add value to a project, team, or codebase — a key word for describing past work.
Finding non-obvious solutions by combining technical and design thinking.
Working across multiple disciplines simultaneously — design, code, and UX at once.
The process of choosing between options — often what interviewers probe with behavioral questions.
A thorough, detailed investigation into a specific problem or topic.
Clarifying what the actual issue is before jumping to solutions — a key engineering and design skill.
To complete and ship a working result — designs, features, or documentation.
An instinctive awareness of what looks, feels, and functions well in a UI.
Attentive to small specifics — spacing, edge cases, naming — that affect overall quality.
To write clear records of decisions, processes, or code so others can understand and build on them.
To lead or push something forward proactively — projects, decisions, or improvements.
A rehearsal or test run before the real thing — useful for presentations or demos.
Covering the full scope of a process — from initial design concept to shipped production code.
Approaching problems systematically, thinking about scalability, edge cases, and constraints.
To promote and build enthusiasm for a practice, tool, or approach within a team.
To try new tools, approaches, or ideas in a controlled way to learn and validate.
Someone who acquires new skills and knowledge quickly, especially in new environments.
Using input from others — users, teammates, reviewers — to guide and improve your work.
To solve problems independently by researching, experimenting, and reasoning through them.
Highly proficient in a tool, language, or methodology — stronger than 'familiar with'.
Your default, preferred tool or approach for a given type of problem.
The belief that skills and intelligence can be developed through effort and learning.
Directly involved in doing the work rather than just directing or planning it.
To take on different roles simultaneously — common in startups and small teams.
To draw attention to the most important or impressive parts of your experience.
Considering the full picture — user needs, technical constraints, and business goals together.
Prioritizing work based on the value it delivers to users or the business.
Code or features that are live and being used by real users — not just prototypes.
To act proactively without being asked — identifying problems and proposing solutions.
A phrase to express genuine motivation for a role, team, or problem space.
A natural, trained ability to notice specific qualities — detail, proportion, inconsistency.
To guide a project, decision, or team — even informally without a formal title.
To embrace and commit to something rather than avoid it — a strength, a challenge, a direction.
Code or systems that are easy to update, extend, and understand by other developers.
To guide and support someone less experienced in developing their skills.
Organizing work around concrete, measurable checkpoints rather than open-ended efforts.
What drives your energy and engagement — a key question in culture-fit interviews.
To execute the small specifics with high precision — spacing, states, edge cases.
Taking full responsibility for a task or area — from start to finish, including problems.
A phrase expressing genuine enthusiasm — use sparingly and always back it with specifics.
Focused on practical solutions that work rather than theoretically perfect ones.
Anticipating problems and acting before being asked rather than reacting after the fact.
Documented history of successfully delivering results — backed by portfolio and examples.
Delivering quality work in a short amount of time without sacrificing standards.
Improving something incrementally through iteration rather than getting it perfect at once.
Equipped with the habits, tools, and communication skills to work effectively in a remote team.
The measurable or observable outcomes of your work — what actually changed because of you.
Able to identify what needs to be done and pursue it without waiting for instructions.
Quick to understand new concepts, catch issues, and find clever solutions — a compliment in tech.
To release and deploy a feature or product to real users — the ultimate goal of any dev work.
A personal project built outside of work — often the best signal of genuine passion in tech.
Strong fundamental knowledge in a skill that allows you to build more advanced work on top.
To address or provide evidence for something — used when backing up a claim with an example.
The skills and qualities where you consistently perform above average — know yours cold.
Someone who breaks down complex problems into clear, logical steps before acting.
To care deeply about the quality of your work — not just getting it done but getting it right.
A thorough understanding of how something works beneath the surface, not just how to use it.
To perform exceptionally well in a particular environment or type of challenge.
To convert design intent into working code, or technical constraints into design language.
What specifically makes you different from other candidates applying for the same role.
Making decisions based on actual user needs and behaviors rather than assumptions.
Capable of adapting across different roles, tools, and types of problems effectively.
A clear picture of where you want to take something — a product, a system, or your career.
An interviewer's request to explain a project or process step by step in your own words.
Something actively being developed — honest framing when showing unfinished portfolio work.
Shorthand for tenure — but quality and depth of experience matter more than raw years.
Feedback or information specific enough to act on immediately without further clarification.
Shared understanding and agreement among team members on goals, priorities, or direction.
Unclear requirements or situations where multiple interpretations are possible.
Exchanging information without expecting an immediate response, common in remote teams.
A person's available time and mental capacity to take on additional work.
Spending disproportionate time debating trivial details while ignoring more important decisions.
An obstacle preventing progress on a task that requires external resolution to move forward.
Standardized, reusable text or code used repeatedly with little or no modification.
A point in a workflow where progress slows because demand exceeds capacity.
Genuine support and commitment from stakeholders or team members for a decision or initiative.
The regular rhythm of recurring meetings, releases, or communication cycles in a team.
The total amount of work a team or individual can realistically handle in a given period.
A warning or qualification that limits or conditions a statement or decision.
A curated log documenting notable changes, fixes, and new features across product versions.
The quality of communication that leaves no room for misinterpretation or confusion.
The meeting point of multiple ideas, teams, or processes — also a common team wiki tool.
General agreement reached by a group, though not necessarily unanimous approval.
Shifting mental focus between different tasks or projects, which reduces deep work quality.
Involving members from different disciplines — design, engineering, product — working together.
A tangible output — a design, document, or feature — produced for a project or client.
The measurable difference or change between two states, versions, or data points.
A task, team, or system that another piece of work relies on before it can proceed.
A research phase aimed at understanding user needs and constraints before designing solutions.
Using your own product internally before releasing it to identify issues firsthand.
Raising an unresolved issue to a higher authority when it cannot be solved at the current level.
An executive or senior leader — often the audience for high-level presentations and summaries.
The scope or impact of a system, feature, or team within an organization or codebase.
Unnecessary resistance in a process, workflow, or user experience that slows progress.
Comparing the current state to the desired state to identify what needs to change.
To gather or accumulate something over time, such as feedback, support, or data.
Designed to be understood at a quick look without sustained attention or reading.
Broken into fine, detailed levels of control or specificity rather than broad categories.
A rule or constraint put in place to prevent teams from making harmful or inconsistent decisions.
A value embedded directly in code or a design rather than stored in a configurable token or variable.
An informal advance warning given to someone before something happens.
Describing a broad overview of something without going into implementation details.
The measurable effect a decision, feature, or change has on users or business outcomes.
A consequence or indirect effect that follows from a decision without being explicitly stated.
Focused on excessive detail at the expense of the bigger picture or strategic thinking.
A structured process for receiving, reviewing, and triaging new requests or projects.
Specialized terminology used within a field that may be unclear to outsiders.
The initial meeting that formally starts a project, aligning the team on goals and expectations.
To use an existing resource, system, or advantage strategically to maximize output.
Easy wins requiring minimal effort that can be completed quickly for fast visible impact.
A significant checkpoint in a project timeline marking the completion of a key phase.
To point out very minor issues in code or design, often during review — sometimes labeled as such.
A guiding metric or vision that defines long-term success for a product or team.
An unresolved question or task that was raised but never followed up on or closed.
A tool or system that enforces specific conventions and limits how much users can customize.
Extra work, time, or cost required beyond the core task — often from process or tooling.
To take full responsibility for a task, decision, or outcome within a team context.
A recurring problem experienced by a user, customer, or team that drives the need for a solution.
A documented record of decisions, communications, or changes kept for reference or accountability.
A list of topics raised in a meeting that are deferred to be discussed later.
To make a significant strategic change in direction in response to new information or constraints.
A retrospective analysis of a project, incident, or failure to extract learnings without blame.
A planning exercise imagining a project has failed to identify risks before they occur.
Resistance or disagreement expressed in response to a proposal or decision.
A tangent or deeply nested problem that consumes time without advancing the main goal.
The period during which a new team member or system gradually reaches full productivity.
The reasoning or justification behind a design decision, technical choice, or strategy.
To improve something incrementally by removing rough edges or adjusting based on feedback.
A recurring team meeting reflecting on what went well, what didn't, and what to improve next.
A significant obstacle preventing a team from moving forward on a project or goal.
The staged or full release of a feature or system to users or internal teams.
Explaining a problem aloud to an object or person to clarify your own thinking and find the solution.
A quick informal verification that something makes sense before committing further resources.
The gradual, uncontrolled expansion of a project's requirements beyond its original boundaries.
Formal approval from a stakeholder or decision-maker to proceed with a design or plan.
A time-boxed research task undertaken to reduce uncertainty before committing to a solution.
To bring a hidden issue, insight, or risk into visibility so the team can address it.
Communicating difficult feedback or disagreement with sensitivity and awareness of others' feelings.
Accumulated shortcuts or suboptimal code decisions that will require future rework.
To set a fixed maximum duration for a task or discussion to prevent it from overrunning.
Early evidence that a product, feature, or initiative is gaining real user adoption or momentum.
A situation where gaining one benefit requires accepting a cost or limitation elsewhere.
To prioritize a list of bugs, tasks, or requests by urgency and impact to decide what to tackle first.
The rate at which team members leave and are replaced, affecting knowledge continuity.
To resolve whatever is preventing a teammate or task from moving forward.
Using more words than necessary — a quality to avoid in both writing and code.
How transparent or accessible the status of a project, decision, or metric is to the team.
A temporary solution that bypasses a problem without permanently fixing the root cause.
Doing a chain of seemingly necessary but tangential tasks before reaching the original goal.
A sentence structure where the subject performs the action, making writing clearer and more direct.
A callout block — note, warning, tip — that draws attention to important supplementary information.
Linking to a specific section within a document using a URL fragment identifier.
Documentation describing every endpoint, parameter, and response of an API exhaustively.
Tailoring technical content to the specific knowledge level and goals of its intended readers.
A visually distinct block highlighting critical information separate from the main body text.
A single versioned record describing what changed, was fixed, or was added in a release.
Inline text within source code explaining why something is done, not just what it does.
Triple backticks in Markdown that wrap a code block and optionally declare its language.
Informal or conversational language that may be unclear or culturally specific to some readers.
Expressing information using the fewest words necessary without losing meaning or accuracy.
A defined category of documentation — tutorial, how-to, reference, explanation — each with a distinct purpose.
A link or pointer directing readers to related content in another section or document.
A documented warning that a feature or API will be removed in a future version.
A documentation framework organizing content into tutorials, how-tos, references, and explanations.
Clarifying which of multiple possible meanings or uses of a term is intended in a given context.
A structured comment inside a function or class that describes its purpose, parameters, and return value.
Treating documentation like source code — versioned in Git, reviewed via PRs, and built automatically.
Explicit documentation of unusual or boundary scenarios a system handles or intentionally excludes.
Documentation written to remain accurate over time without frequent updates tied to a specific version.
A piece of documentation focused on building conceptual understanding rather than guiding a task.
Metadata at the top of a Markdown file — title, date, tags — parsed by static site generators.
An alphabetical list of domain-specific terms with their definitions within a documentation site.
A goal-oriented document walking a competent user through steps to complete a specific task.
A writing style using command verbs — click, open, run — standard in step-by-step instructions.
The cues — links, headings, labels — that help readers predict whether following a path will pay off.
Monospace-formatted text within a sentence used to reference code values, filenames, or commands.
A JavaScript documentation standard using tagged comments to describe functions and types.
A searchable collection of articles and guides helping users solve problems independently.
Adapting content for a specific locale — language, date formats, cultural references — beyond translation.
The opening sentence that summarizes the most critical information, front-loading the key point.
A lightweight markup language using plain-text syntax to produce formatted HTML documents.
An extension of Markdown that allows embedding React components directly inside documentation files.
Short UI text — labels, error messages, tooltips, placeholders — that guides users through an interface.
The visible text of a link or menu item that sets reader expectations about the destination.
A string of multiple nouns used as modifiers that creates ambiguous, hard-to-parse phrases.
Guides designed to help new users or developers reach their first success as quickly as possible.
A specification format for describing REST APIs in a machine-readable YAML or JSON file.
A sentence structure where the subject receives the action — often vaguer and harder to follow.
Writing that communicates clearly to its intended audience without unnecessary complexity.
A condition or setup step that must be completed before following a guide or tutorial.
Structuring documentation to reveal complexity gradually as the reader advances.
Ordinary written language as opposed to structured formats like bullet lists or tables.
The entry-point document of a repository describing its purpose, setup, and usage.
User-facing documentation summarizing what changed, improved, or was fixed in a product release.
A step-by-step operational guide for handling a specific incident or routine system procedure.
The ease with which a reader can skim a document and extract the most relevant information.
Writing content once and reusing it across multiple documents or output formats.
A short, self-contained code example illustrating a specific concept or API usage.
A written document detailing the requirements, behavior, and constraints of a feature or system.
A document defining writing conventions — voice, tone, terminology — for consistent communication.
A navigational list of headings linking to sections within a long document.
The degree to which documentation correctly reflects the actual behavior of a system.
The specific set of terms defined and used consistently across a product and its documentation.
Abbreviation for table of contents — often auto-generated from document headings in doc tools.
The personality and attitude expressed through writing — formal, friendly, direct, empathetic.
A learning-oriented document guiding a beginner through a complete working example step by step.
The practice of crafting all interface text — labels, errors, onboarding — to guide user behavior.
Maintaining separate documentation sets for each major release of a product or API.
The consistent personality and character expressed across all written content for a product or brand.
A guided explanation of a process or codebase, often used in reviews or onboarding sessions.
A vague qualifier — easily, simply, just — that implies something is straightforward when it may not be.
The deliberate discipline of achieving maximum clarity with the minimum number of words.
A property of an object that signals how it can be interacted with.
The structural breakdown of a UI component into its named constituent parts.
Notes added to design files to explain decisions or specs for developer handoff.
Design practice ensuring products are usable by people with a range of disabilities.
A visual audit listing all interactive elements and their intended perceived actions.
A research method where users group topics into categories that feel logical to them.
Grouping related content together so users can process information more efficiently.
The total mental effort required to process and interact with a user interface.
The measured difference in luminance between two colors, used to evaluate accessibility.
The order in which information is presented to guide user attention and comprehension.
A research method measuring emotional and aesthetic reactions to a design.
Accumulated inconsistencies or shortcuts in a design that need future correction.
The process of transferring finalized designs and specs from designer to developer.
A structured session where designers give and receive specific, constructive feedback.
The ease with which users can find features or content they did not know existed.
An unusual or extreme scenario that falls outside typical user behavior patterns.
The UI state shown when there is no content to display, often used to guide users.
A UI condition that communicates something went wrong and guides the user to recover.
The level of detail and realism of a prototype, ranging from low to high.
A model predicting that target acquisition time depends on the target's size and distance.
The sequence of steps a user takes to complete a task within a product.
Perceptual principles describing how the brain organizes visual elements into unified wholes.
A button with a transparent fill and only an outline border, typically a secondary action.
A mathematical proportion (~1.618) applied in design to achieve visually balanced layouts.
A structure of columns, rows, and gutters used to align and organize interface elements.
Informal usability testing conducted quickly in public spaces with random participants.
A practical usability principle used to evaluate and identify interface problems.
An expert review of a UI measured against established usability principles.
Visual structure that signals the relative importance of elements to guide user attention.
A defined clickable area in a prototype that triggers navigation or an interaction.
The structural design of shared information environments to support usability and findability.
The practice of designing how users interact with digital products over time.
A cyclic design process of prototyping, testing, and refining based on user feedback.
A framework focusing on the underlying goal a user is trying to accomplish with a product.
A visualization of the end-to-end experience a user has with a product over time.
How quickly a new user can become proficient at using an interface.
A user's internal representation of how a system works based on prior experience.
A small, contained product moment that accomplishes a single task with user feedback.
A dialog overlay that requires user interaction before returning to the main interface.
Minimum Viable Product — a version with just enough features to test a core hypothesis.
A reusable structural approach for helping users move through an interface.
The process of guiding new users to understand and adopt a product's core value.
A specific problem or frustration a user experiences when interacting with a product.
A design approach that actively involves users as co-creators in the design process.
A fictional but research-based character representing a key user segment of a product.
Revealing information gradually to reduce complexity and avoid overwhelming the user.
A simulation of a product used to test ideas before committing to development.
A gestalt principle stating that elements placed close together are perceived as related.
The critical tasks users perform most frequently that must work flawlessly in a product.
A design approach where layouts adapt fluidly to different screen sizes and devices.
Temporary UI support structures that guide users until they no longer need assistance.
The ease with which users can quickly read and extract key information from a page.
How easily a product can be maintained, updated, or corrected after deployment.
A visual or auditory cue that communicates how an object or element should be used.
A placeholder UI that mimics the layout of content while data is loading.
A design style where digital elements mimic the appearance of real-world counterparts.
A UI element that remains fixed in the viewport as the user scrolls the page.
A linear sequence of steps a user follows to complete one specific task.
A usability method where participants verbalize their thoughts while using a product.
A brief, non-blocking notification that appears temporarily to confirm an action.
A small label that appears on hover to provide additional context for an element.
A research method evaluating how easily users find items within a navigation structure.
Observing real users attempting tasks to identify friction points in a product.
A diagram showing all possible paths a user can take through a product.
A short description of a feature from the user's perspective: As a… I want… So that…
The degree to which a product provides features that fulfill real user needs.
Real-time feedback confirming that a user's input meets the required format or criteria.
The perceived heaviness of a design element based on size, color, contrast, and position.
Design strategies that help users understand where they are and how to navigate a system.
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines — international standards for accessible digital content.
Empty space in a layout used intentionally to improve readability and visual balance.
A low-fidelity schematic showing the structure and layout of a screen without visual design.
A multi-step UI pattern that breaks a complex task into sequential, manageable steps.
The first-time experience when a user has no data yet — often an onboarding opportunity.
A reading pattern where eyes scan across the top, diagonally down, then across the bottom.
A common reading pattern where users scan horizontally at top, then skim down the left side.
The portion of a webpage visible without scrolling, considered prime real estate.
A secondary navigation trail showing the user's current location within a site hierarchy.
A UI element that prompts users to take a specific desired action, often a button.
A deceptive UI design that tricks users into actions they did not intend to take.
A cycle where system responses to user actions inform and shape subsequent behavior.
